---
title: "ISA 401: Business Intelligence & Data Visualization"
subtitle: '08: Images & Documents in `r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("r-project"), fill = "white")`'
author: '<br>Fadel M. Megahed, PhD <br><br>Raymond E. Gloss Professor in Business <br> Farmer School of Business<br> Miami University<br><br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("linkedin"), fill = "white")` @Fadel Megahed](https://www.linkedin.com/in/fadel-megahed-289046b4) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("github"), fill = "white")` fmegahed](https://github.com/fmegahed/) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("paper-plane", style = "solid"), fill = "white")` fmegahed@miamioh.edu](mailto:fmegahed@miamioh.edu)<br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("question"), fill = "white")` Automated Scheduler for Office Hours](https://calendly.com/fmegahed)<br><br>'
date: "Spring 2026"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    self_contained: true
    css: [default, "../../style_files/fonts.css", "../../style_files/my-theme.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: magula
      highlightLines: true
      highlightLanguage: ["r"]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: "16:9"
header-includes:
  - "../../style_files/header.html"
---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = FALSE,
                      echo = TRUE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      progress = FALSE,
                      verbose = FALSE,
                      dev = 'png',
                      fig.height = 2.5,
                      dpi = 300,
                      fig.align = 'center')

options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)

miamired = '#C3142D'

if(require(pacman)==FALSE) install.packages("pacman")
if(require(devtools)==FALSE) install.packages("devtools")

if(require(countdown)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/countdown")
if(require(xaringanExtra)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/xaringanExtra")


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, magrittr, lubridate, janitor, # data analysis pkgs
               pdftools, httr2, jsonlite, base64enc, # for PDFs and APIs
               fontawesome, RefManageR, xaringanExtra, countdown) # for slides
```

```{r xaringan-themer, include=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
if(require(xaringanthemer) == FALSE) install.packages("xaringanthemer")
library(xaringanthemer)

xaringanthemer::style_mono_accent(base_color = "#84d6d3",
                  base_font_size = "20px")

xaringanExtra::use_xaringan_extra(c("tile_view", "animate_css", "tachyons", "panelset", "search", "fit_screen", "editable",
                                    "clipable"))
```


## Quick Refresher from Last Class

`r emo::ji("check")` Use LLM APIs to extract structured data from unstructured text

`r emo::ji("check")` Apply prompt engineering techniques for consistent JSON output

`r emo::ji("check")` Parse LLM responses into tidy data frames in `r fontawesome::fa(name = 'r-project', fill = miamired)`


---

## Kahoot/Quiz Review

Test your understanding of prompt engineering and JSON parsing:

1. What role does the **system prompt** play when calling an LLM API for structured extraction?

2. Why is it important to specify the **exact JSON schema** you want in your prompt?

3. When parsing a nested JSON response from an LLM, which `r fontawesome::fa(name = 'r-project', fill = miamired)` function(s) help flatten it into a data frame?

4. What happens if the LLM returns **malformed JSON** -- how should your code handle it?

5. Name one advantage of using **few-shot examples** in your extraction prompt.

`r countdown::countdown(minutes = 5, warn_when = 60, top = 0)`


---

## Learning Objectives for Today's Class

- **LO1:** Extract tabular data from PDF documents

- **LO2:** Use vision APIs to analyze images

- **LO3:** Extract structured information from infographics


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Extracting Data from PDFs


---

## The Problem: Data Trapped in PDFs

.pull-left[
Many organizations publish critical data **only as PDF reports**:

- Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) salary reports
- SEC financial filings
- Government census tables
- Annual reports and whitepapers

The data is **visible** but not **accessible** for analysis.
]

.pull-right[
```{r bls-pdf-example, echo=FALSE, out.width='95%', fig.cap='Example: BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics are often distributed as PDF tables.'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://www.bls.gov/oes/images/oes_brochure_2021.png")
```
]


---

## The pdftools Package: Extracting Text

The [`pdftools`](https://docs.ropensci.org/pdftools/) `r fontawesome::fa('box', fill = 'gold')` package provides `pdf_text()` to extract raw text from PDF files.

```{r pdftools-demo, eval=FALSE}
# Extract text from a PDF (one string per page)
pdf_url <- "https://www.bls.gov/news.release/pdf/ocwage.pdf"
pdf_pages <- pdftools::pdf_text(pdf_url)

# How many pages?
length(pdf_pages)

# View first page text
cat(pdf_pages[1])
```

**Key insight:** `pdf_text()` returns a **character vector** where each element is one page of text. Tables appear as whitespace-aligned text -- useful but not structured.


---

## pdftools: Additional Utilities

Beyond `pdf_text()`, the `pdftools` package provides several helpful functions:

```{r pdftools-utils, eval=FALSE}
# Get metadata about the PDF
pdftools::pdf_info(pdf_url)  # author, creation date, number of pages, etc.

# Extract table of contents (if present)
pdftools::pdf_toc(pdf_url)

# Get the number of pages
pdftools::pdf_length(pdf_url)

# Render a PDF page as an image (useful for vision APIs later!)
pdftools::pdf_render_page(pdf_url, page = 1, dpi = 150) |>
  png::writePNG("page1.png")
```

**Pro tip:** `pdf_render_page()` converts PDF pages to images -- a bridge between PDF extraction and vision API extraction that we will use later.


---

## pdftools: From Text to Data

Once you have raw text, you can use string processing to extract tables:

```{r pdftools-parse, eval=FALSE}
# Extract text from a specific page
page_text <- pdf_pages[2]

# Split into lines
lines <- strsplit(page_text, "\n")[[1]]

# Filter lines that look like table rows (contain numbers)
table_lines <- lines[grepl("\\d{2,}", lines)]

# Parse fixed-width columns using readr::read_fwf or string manipulation
salary_data <- readr::read_fwf(
  I(paste(table_lines, collapse = "\n")),
  col_positions = readr::fwf_empty(
    I(paste(table_lines, collapse = "\n"))
  )
)

head(salary_data)
```

**Limitation:** This approach requires manual tuning for each PDF layout.


---

## tabulapdf: Purpose-Built Table Extraction

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[About tabulapdf]

The [`tabulapdf`](https://docs.ropensci.org/tabulapdf/) `r fontawesome::fa('box', fill = 'gold')` package wraps the Java-based Tabula library, which is specifically designed to extract **tables** from PDFs.

- Automatically detects table boundaries
- Returns data frames directly
- Handles multi-page tables
- Requires Java (install via `install.packages("tabulapdf")`)

```{r tabula-install, eval=FALSE}
if(require(tabulapdf) == FALSE) install.packages("tabulapdf")
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Basic Usage]

```{r tabula-demo, eval=FALSE}
# Extract all tables from a PDF
pdf_url <- "https://www.bls.gov/news.release/pdf/ocwage.pdf"
tables <- tabulapdf::extract_tables(pdf_url)

# tables is a list of data frames -- one per detected table
length(tables)

# Inspect the first extracted table
tables[[1]] |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  head(10)
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Targeted Extraction]

```{r tabula-targeted, eval=FALSE}
# Extract from specific pages only
tables_p2 <- tabulapdf::extract_tables(
  pdf_url,
  pages = 2,
  method = "lattice"  # "lattice" for bordered tables, "stream" for borderless
)

# Clean column names and convert types
salary_df <- tables_p2[[1]] |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(dplyr::where(is.character) & !occupation,
                  ~ as.numeric(gsub("[,$]", "", .x)))
  )

salary_df
```

]

]


---

## Comparing Approaches: pdftools vs tabulapdf

| Feature | `pdftools::pdf_text()` | `tabulapdf::extract_tables()` |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| **Output** | Raw text (character) | Data frames |
| **Best for** | Free-form text, paragraphs | Structured tables |
| **Dependencies** | Minimal (C library) | Requires Java |
| **Table detection** | Manual parsing needed | Automatic |
| **Accuracy** | Depends on parsing | High for well-formatted tables |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Moderate |

**Rule of thumb:** Start with `tabulapdf` for tables. Fall back to `pdftools` + string parsing when tables are irregular.


---

## Activity: Extract Salary Data from a BLS PDF

.font90[
**Your task:** Extract the occupational salary data from a BLS PDF report.

**Steps:**

1. Download or link to a BLS PDF (e.g., `"https://www.bls.gov/news.release/pdf/ocwage.pdf"`)

2. Try **both** approaches:
   - `pdftools::pdf_text()` -- examine the raw text output
   - `tabulapdf::extract_tables()` -- extract structured tables

3. Clean the resulting data frame:
   - Apply `janitor::clean_names()`
   - Convert salary columns to numeric

4. Answer: What is the **median annual wage** for "Computer and Mathematical" occupations?
]

`r countdown::countdown(minutes = 5, warn_when = 60, top = 0)`


---

## Section Check-In: PDF Extraction

`r emo::ji("check")` You can use `pdftools::pdf_text()` to extract raw text from PDFs

`r emo::ji("check")` You can use `tabulapdf::extract_tables()` to pull structured tables

`r emo::ji("check")` You know when to use each approach

**Up next:** What about data locked inside *images*?


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Seeing with AI: Vision APIs


---

## How Vision APIs Work

.pull-left[
Traditional workflow for image data:

1. **Human** looks at an image
2. **Human** manually types numbers into a spreadsheet
3. Error-prone, slow, does not scale

AI Vision workflow:

1. **Send** image to a vision-capable LLM
2. **Receive** structured text/JSON description
3. Automated, scalable, auditable
]

.pull-right[
```{r vision-flow, echo=FALSE, out.width='100%'}
# Conceptual diagram placeholder
knitr::include_graphics("https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Cat03.jpg/1200px-Cat03.jpg")
```

.center[.font80[**Send image** `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right")` **API processes** `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right")` **Get structured data back**]]
]


---

## Encoding Images for API Requests

Vision APIs expect images as **base64-encoded strings**. The `base64enc` `r fontawesome::fa('box', fill = 'gold')` package handles this:

```{r base64-demo, eval=FALSE}
# Option 1: Encode a local file
img_raw <- readBin("chart.png", "raw", file.info("chart.png")$size)
img_base64 <- base64enc::base64encode(img_raw)

# Option 2: Encode from a URL (download first)
tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".png")
download.file("https://example.com/chart.png", tmp, mode = "wb")
img_raw <- readBin(tmp, "raw", file.info(tmp)$size)
img_base64 <- base64enc::base64encode(img_raw)

# The result is a long string like "iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUg..."
nchar(img_base64)  # typically thousands of characters
```

**Why base64?** APIs communicate via JSON, which is text-based. Binary image data must be encoded as text to embed in the request body.


---

## Building a Vision API Request with httr2

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Request Structure]

To send an image to the Claude API for analysis, we need:

1. **API Key** stored in `.Renviron` as `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
2. **Base64-encoded image** embedded in the message
3. **A prompt** telling the model what to extract

```{r vision-structure, eval=FALSE}
# Ensure your API key is set
# usethis::edit_r_environ("project")
# Add: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

api_key <- Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Building the Request]

```{r vision-request, eval=FALSE}
# Build the API request
req <- httr2::request("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages") |>
  httr2::req_method("POST") |>
  httr2::req_headers(
    `x-api-key` = api_key,
    `anthropic-version` = "2023-06-01",
    `content-type` = "application/json"
  ) |>
  httr2::req_body_json(list(
    model = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens = 1024,
    messages = list(list(
      role = "user",
      content = list(
        list(type = "image", source = list(
          type = "base64", media_type = "image/png", data = img_base64
        )),
        list(type = "text", text = "Extract all data from this chart as a JSON array of objects with keys: category, value.")
      )
    ))
  ))
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Parsing the Response]

```{r vision-response, eval=FALSE}
# Perform the request
resp <- httr2::req_perform(req)

# Extract the text response
result <- resp |>
  httr2::resp_body_json()

# The model's text response
model_text <- result$content[[1]]$text

# Parse the JSON from the response
extracted_data <- jsonlite::fromJSON(model_text)

# Convert to a tibble
extracted_df <- tibble::as_tibble(extracted_data)
extracted_df
```

]

]


---

## Demo: Analyzing an Infographic with Claude Vision

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[The Task]

We will send a BLS infographic to Claude and ask it to extract the underlying data as a structured JSON table.

**Goal:** Go from a *picture of data* to a *data frame* we can analyze.

```{r demo-infographic-url, eval=FALSE}
# Example: a BLS employment infographic
img_url <- "https://www.bls.gov/ooh/images/15702.png"

# Download and encode
tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".png")
download.file(img_url, tmp, mode = "wb")
img_raw <- readBin(tmp, "raw", file.info(tmp)$size)
img_b64 <- base64enc::base64encode(img_raw)
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[API Call]

```{r demo-api-call, eval=FALSE}
req <- httr2::request("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages") |>
  httr2::req_method("POST") |>
  httr2::req_headers(
    `x-api-key` = Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"),
    `anthropic-version` = "2023-06-01",
    `content-type` = "application/json"
  ) |>
  httr2::req_body_json(list(
    model = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens = 2048,
    messages = list(list(
      role = "user",
      content = list(
        list(type = "image", source = list(
          type = "base64", media_type = "image/png", data = img_b64
        )),
        list(type = "text", text = "Extract ALL data shown in this infographic. Return a JSON array of objects. Each object should have descriptive keys matching the labels in the image. Include ALL numbers, percentages, and categories visible.")
      )
    ))
  ))

resp <- httr2::req_perform(req)
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Results]

```{r demo-results, eval=FALSE}
# Parse the response
result <- resp |> httr2::resp_body_json()
model_text <- result$content[[1]]$text

# Extract JSON from the response (model may wrap it in markdown)
json_str <- stringr::str_extract(model_text, "\\[.*\\]")

# Parse into a data frame
infographic_df <- jsonlite::fromJSON(json_str) |>
  tibble::as_tibble()

infographic_df |>
  knitr::kable(format = "html")
```

]

]


---

## Prompt Tips for Vision Extraction

When sending images to an LLM for data extraction, your prompt matters:

.pull-left[
**Do:**
- Specify the **exact output format** (JSON schema)
- Ask for **ALL** visible data, not just "some"
- Mention expected data types (numbers, percentages)
- Request keys that match the image labels
- Use few-shot examples for complex layouts
]

.pull-right[
**Avoid:**
- Vague prompts like "describe this image"
- Asking for interpretation instead of extraction
- Forgetting to handle markdown-wrapped JSON
- Assuming the model will infer your schema
]

**Example prompt:**
> "Extract all data from this bar chart. Return a JSON array where each object has keys: `occupation` (string), `median_salary` (number), `employment_count` (number)."


---

## Handling Errors and Edge Cases

Vision API calls can fail or return unexpected results. Defensive coding is essential:

```{r error-handling, eval=FALSE}
# Wrap API calls in tryCatch for robustness
safe_extract <- function(image_path, prompt) {
  tryCatch({
    result <- extract_chart_data(image_path, prompt)
    if(nrow(result) == 0) {
      warning("API returned empty data frame")
      return(tibble::tibble())
    }
    result
  }, error = function(e) {
    message("API call failed: ", e$message)
    tibble::tibble()  # return empty tibble on failure
  })
}

# Common issues to watch for:
# 1. API rate limits (429 errors) -- add httr2::req_retry()
# 2. Model returns text instead of JSON -- use regex extraction
# 3. Image too large -- resize before encoding
# 4. Hallucinated values -- always validate against the source
```


---

## Section Check-In: Vision APIs

`r emo::ji("check")` You understand how to encode images as base64 for API requests

`r emo::ji("check")` You can build a vision API request using `httr2` to the Claude API

`r emo::ji("check")` You can parse structured data from the model's response

**Up next:** Putting it all together -- recovering data from charts and infographics.


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Recovering Data from Charts


---

## Chart Data Recovery: The Motivation

.font90[
Data visualization is powerful -- but sometimes the **original data is lost** and only the chart remains:

- A colleague shares a **screenshot** of a dashboard
- A report contains a **bar chart** but no underlying CSV
- A news article includes an **infographic** with no data download

**Vision APIs let you reverse-engineer the data from the image.**
]


---

## Combining PDF + Vision: Scanned Documents

Some PDFs are **scanned images** -- `pdf_text()` returns empty strings because there is no text layer. Combine `pdftools` with the vision API:

```{r scanned-doc, eval=FALSE}
# Step 1: Render the PDF page as an image
img_bitmap <- pdftools::pdf_render_page("scanned_report.pdf", page = 1, dpi = 200)
png::writePNG(img_bitmap, "scanned_page1.png")

# Step 2: Encode and send to vision API
scanned_df <- extract_chart_data(
  image_path = "scanned_page1.png",
  prompt = "This is a scanned table from a government report. Extract all rows
  and columns as a JSON array. Each object should have keys matching the
  column headers visible in the table."
)

# Step 3: Clean the result
scanned_df |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(dplyr::where(is.character), stringr::str_trim))
```

**This is the ultimate fallback:** when no other tool can parse the PDF, render it as an image and use AI vision.


---

## Example: Bar Chart to Data Frame

.pull-left[
**Before:** An image of a bar chart

```{r bar-chart-example, echo=FALSE, fig.height=4, fig.width=5, dpi=150}
# Create a sample chart to demonstrate
sample_data <- tibble::tibble(
  occupation = c("Software Dev", "Data Analyst",
                 "Accountant", "Nurse", "Teacher"),
  median_salary = c(120730, 82360, 77250, 81220, 61690)
)

ggplot2::ggplot(sample_data, ggplot2::aes(x = reorder(occupation, median_salary),
                         y = median_salary)) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(fill = "#84d6d3") +
  ggplot2::geom_text(ggplot2::aes(label = scales::dollar(median_salary)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3) +
  ggplot2::coord_flip() +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar, limits = c(0, 150000)) +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "Median Annual Salary by Occupation",
       x = NULL, y = "Median Salary") +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal()
```
]

.pull-right[
**After:** A tidy data frame extracted via Vision API

| occupation | median_salary |
|:-----------|-------------:|
| Software Dev | 120,730 |
| Data Analyst | 82,360 |
| Accountant | 77,250 |
| Nurse | 81,220 |
| Teacher | 61,690 |

This data can now be joined, filtered, aggregated, and visualized in `r fontawesome::fa(name = 'r-project', fill = miamired)`.
]


---

## Building a Reusable Extraction Function

```{r extract-function, eval=FALSE}
extract_chart_data <- function(image_path, prompt, api_key = Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")) {
  # Encode image
  img_raw <- readBin(image_path, "raw", file.info(image_path)$size)
  img_b64 <- base64enc::base64encode(img_raw)
  ext <- tools::file_ext(image_path)
  media_type <- paste0("image/", ifelse(ext == "jpg", "jpeg", ext))

  # Build and send request
  resp <- httr2::request("https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages") |>
    httr2::req_method("POST") |>
    httr2::req_headers(`x-api-key` = api_key, `anthropic-version` = "2023-06-01",
                       `content-type` = "application/json") |>
    httr2::req_body_json(list(
      model = "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", max_tokens = 2048,
      messages = list(list(role = "user", content = list(
        list(type = "image", source = list(type = "base64", media_type = media_type, data = img_b64)),
        list(type = "text", text = prompt)
      )))
    )) |> httr2::req_perform()

  # Parse response
  model_text <- httr2::resp_body_json(resp)$content[[1]]$text
  json_str <- stringr::str_extract(model_text, "\\[.*\\]")
  jsonlite::fromJSON(json_str) |> tibble::as_tibble()
}
```


---

## Using the Extraction Function

```{r use-function, eval=FALSE}
# Extract data from a saved chart image
salary_df <- extract_chart_data(
  image_path = "bls_salary_chart.png",
  prompt = "Extract all data from this bar chart. Return a JSON array where
  each object has keys: occupation (string), median_salary (number)."
)

# Now we can work with it like any data frame
salary_df |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(median_salary)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(salary_formatted = scales::dollar(median_salary))
```

**Benefits of wrapping this in a function:**
- Reusable across projects
- Easy to modify the prompt per use case
- Handles encoding and parsing in one call


---

## Validating Extracted Data

.font90[
Vision API extraction is powerful but not perfect. Always **validate** the output:

```{r validate-data, eval=FALSE}
# 1. Check dimensions -- does the number of rows match what you see?
nrow(extracted_df)  # compare to the number of bars/rows in the chart

# 2. Check value ranges -- are numbers plausible?
summary(extracted_df$median_salary)

# 3. Check for missing or NA values
colSums(is.na(extracted_df))

# 4. Cross-reference a few values manually against the source image
# If the chart says "Software Dev: $120,730", does the data frame agree?

# 5. Run the extraction twice and compare (LLM outputs can vary)
run1 <- extract_chart_data("chart.png", prompt)
run2 <- extract_chart_data("chart.png", prompt)
all.equal(run1, run2)
```

**Best practice:** For high-stakes analyses, run the extraction multiple times and take the consensus values. Report any discrepancies.
]


---

## Decision Framework: When to Use What

.font80[

| Scenario | Tool | Why |
|----------|------|-----|
| Data is in a **CSV/Excel file** | `readr`, `readxl` | Direct import -- always prefer structured data |
| Data is on a **webpage** (HTML table) | `rvest` web scraping | Tables are in the DOM, scraping is reliable |
| Data is behind an **API** | `httr2` + `jsonlite` | API returns structured JSON directly |
| Data is in a **PDF table** | `tabulapdf` / `pdftools` | Designed for extracting tables from PDFs |
| Data is in **unstructured text** | LLM text API | LLM can parse natural language into structure |
| Data is in an **image/chart** | LLM vision API | Only way to extract data from pixels |
| Data is in a **scanned document** | LLM vision API | OCR + extraction in one step |

]

**General principle:** Use the **simplest tool** that works. Vision APIs are powerful but slower and costlier than direct parsing.


---

## Decision Framework: A Flowchart

.center[

Is the data in a **structured file** (CSV, Excel, JSON)?
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down")` Yes: Use `readr`, `readxl`, `jsonlite`

Is the data on a **webpage**?
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down")` Yes: Use `rvest` or an API if available

Is the data in a **PDF**?
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down")` Yes: Try `tabulapdf` first, then `pdftools`

Is the data in **unstructured text**?
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down")` Yes: Use an LLM text API with JSON schema

Is the data in an **image or screenshot**?
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down")` Yes: Use an LLM vision API with JSON schema

]


---

## Activity: Extract Data from a BLS Infographic

.font90[
**Your task:** Use the Claude Vision API to extract salary data from a BLS infographic image.

**Steps:**

1. Find a BLS infographic image (e.g., from <https://www.bls.gov/ooh/images/>) or save a screenshot of a BLS chart

2. Encode the image using `base64enc::base64encode()`

3. Send it to the Claude API with a prompt requesting JSON output

4. Parse the JSON response into a tibble

5. Create a simple `ggplot2` visualization of the extracted data

**Bonus:** Use the `extract_chart_data()` function from the earlier slide.
]

`r countdown::countdown(minutes = 8, warn_when = 60, top = 0)`


---

## Job Market Connection

.pull-left[
.font90[
**Why does this matter for your career?**

Many organizations still distribute critical data in formats that resist analysis:

- **Consulting firms** share findings as PDF reports
- **Government agencies** publish data as infographics
- **Executives** share dashboard screenshots in emails
- **Legacy systems** produce reports only as images or PDFs

**You can now unlock all of this data** using the tools from today's lecture.
]
]

.pull-right[
.center[
`r fontawesome::fa("file-pdf", height = "2em", fill = miamired)` PDFs
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down", fill = "#84d6d3")`
`r fontawesome::fa("image", height = "2em", fill = miamired)` Images
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down", fill = "#84d6d3")`
`r fontawesome::fa("table", height = "2em", fill = miamired)` Tidy Data Frames
`r fontawesome::fa("arrow-down", fill = "#84d6d3")`
`r fontawesome::fa("chart-bar", height = "2em", fill = miamired)` Insights
]
]


---

## Section Check-In: Infographic Data Recovery

`r emo::ji("check")` You can extract underlying data from chart images using Vision APIs

`r emo::ji("check")` You can build a reusable extraction function

`r emo::ji("check")` You know when to use scraping vs API vs LLM text vs LLM vision


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Recap


---

## Summary of Main Points

By now, you should be able to do the following:

- **Extract tabular data from PDF documents** using `pdftools` and `tabulapdf`

- **Use vision APIs to analyze images** by encoding images as base64 and sending them to the Claude API via `httr2`

- **Extract structured information from infographics** and recover the underlying data from charts and screenshots


---

## Readings and Resources

.pull-left[
**PDF Extraction:**
- [pdftools: Extracting Text and Data from PDFs](https://docs.ropensci.org/pdftools/)
- [tabulapdf: Extract Tables from PDFs](https://docs.ropensci.org/tabulapdf/)

**Vision APIs:**
- [Anthropic Vision Documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/vision)
- [httr2: Getting Started](https://httr2.r-lib.org/articles/httr2.html)
]

.pull-right[
**Additional Resources:**
- [base64enc Package](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/base64enc/)
- [BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook](https://www.bls.gov/ooh/)
- [R for Data Science: Data Import](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/data-import)
]


---

## Things to Do to Prepare for Next Class

- Review the [pdftools vignette](https://docs.ropensci.org/pdftools/) and try extracting text from a PDF of your choice

- Read the [Anthropic Vision documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/vision) to understand image input options

- Ensure your `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is set in your `.Renviron` file

- **Next up:** Tidy Data and Data Validation -- making sure your extracted data is clean and ready for analysis
